summary: idlidosa disables TLS validation, masquerades as Microsoft WebView2, and installs a 1-minute respawn task
details: When the user runs `idlidosa start`, the package performs several actions that materially harm the installer's host integrity and security posture: (1) sets `process.env.NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED = "0"` at the top of the bundled shared module (dist/shared/index.js:197, dist/shared/index.cjs:246, desktop/dist/daemon.cjs:19365), globally disabling TLS certificate validation for every outbound HTTPS call made by the Electron daemon for the lifetime of the process, silently accepting any cert from any host; (2) on Windows, copies the bundled electron.exe to `node_modules/electron/dist/msedgewebview2.exe` and uses rcedit to rewrite the VersionInfo so FileDescription/CompanyName read "Microsoft Edge WebView2 Runtime" / "Microsoft Corporation" before spawning the daemon (dist/cli/index.js ~L270), then sets `process.title = "Windows Audio Device Graph Isolation"` in cli/guard.cjs to further hide the process from operators and EDR; (3) creates a Scheduled Task named `MicrosoftEdgeWebView2Update` that re-launches the daemon every 1 minute if killed (dist/cli/index.js ~L290), making termination by the host's admin user impractical. The package additionally bundles 9 of the author's own Groq API keys (AES-GCM encrypted under the hardcoded passphrase `pageai-pool-v2` in shared/keys.json) — author self-harm only, but it confirms the package is a packaged AI-cheating overlay that explicitly advertises being "hidden from screen sharing." The combination of TLS-validation-off + Microsoft-impersonating binary + Windows-audio-service process title + every-minute respawn task is deliberate AV/EDR/proctor-tool evasion targeting the installer's own machine.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
